3A_Bibliographic.citation: Assari, S. (2020). American children's screen time: Diminished returns of household income in Black families. Information, 11(11), 538.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: The ABCD participants were 9-11-year-old children who were selected from multiple cities across various states. The eligibility of our analysis was having valid data on all our study variables, including race, family income, and screen time. The analytical sample of this paper was 15,022.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Household income (3-level categorical) and race, with income x race interaction
3C_Exposure.modalities: N/A - exposure is family income, not screen time. Screen time is the OUTCOME.
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Household income, reported by the parent, was a three-level categorical measure of less than 50 K (K = 1000), 50-100 K, and 100+ K. Race was self-identified as black, Asian, mixed or other, and white (reference category).
3C_Exposure.notes: This paper uses screen time as the OUTCOME, not the exposure. The exposure is family income and race. This may not fit the systematic review's scope (screen time as exposure).

3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Screen time was reported by the parents. This measure was for average weekdays and measured in hours. This variable was treated as a continuous measure, with a higher value indicating more screen time.
3D_Outcome.notes: Screen time is the OUTCOME in this paper, not the exposure. Mean weekday screen time = 3.55 hours (SD=3.14).

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Mixed-effects regression with random intercepts for ABCD site, family, and subject; Model 2 includes income x race interaction terms
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: race, ethnicity (Hispanic), sex, marital status, age
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: We used two mixed-effects regression models for multivariable modeling that allowed us to adjust to our data's nested nature. Observations were nested to participants from families who were selected across the sites and states. Model 1 did not have any interaction terms between race and family income. Model 2 included interaction terms between race and family income. In both models, children's screen time was the outcome. The regression coefficient (b), SE, 95% CI, and p-value were reported.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours of weekday screen time; Model 1 (no interaction)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3 Model 1: Income (>=50K and <100K): b=-0.60***, SE=0.08, p<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours of weekday screen time; Model 1 (no interaction)
Hours of weekday screen time; Model 1
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3 Model 1: Race (Black): b=1.32***, SE=0.09, p<0.001

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours; Model 2 interaction - diminished returns of income for Black families
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3 Model 2: Income (>=50K and <100K) x Race (Black): b=0.59**, SE=0.21, p=0.004

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours; Model 2 interaction - diminished returns of income for Black families
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3 Model 2: Income (>100K) x Race (Black): b=0.74**, SE=0.25, p=0.003

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours of weekday screen time; Model 1
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3 Model 1: Race (Asian): b=-0.52**, SE=0.19, p=0.005

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: This was documented by a significant statistical interaction between race and family income on children's screen time.
3G_Reporting_Quality.large_n_verbatim: According to Cohen, a sample size of 15,000 will provide a power of 99% for a regression model with eight terms, which explains 0.005 of the variance (expected R square) and a p-value of 0.05.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The paper mentions the power to detect R-squared of 0.005 but does not connect this to the interpretation of clinical significance. CIs are not reported in Table 3 despite being mentioned in the methods.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: To minimize the black-white health and behavioral gap, it is imperative to address societal and structural barriers beyond SES. These include racism, stratification, injustice, and hopelessness in black communities.
3H_Recommendations.notes: Recommendations are about addressing structural racism rather than screen time reduction per se.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames screen time primarily as a health risk behavior and focuses on how its social determinants differ by race. The main argument is about Minorities' Diminished Returns (MDRs) - that family income has weaker protective effects on screen time for Black children compared to White children. The paper treats screen time as inherently negative (linking it to obesity, depression) without questioning this assumption. The focus is on structural racism as the driver of racial disparities in screen time rather than on screen time effects per se.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Screen time is the OUTCOME in this paper, not the exposure predicting a health outcome. This may not fit the systematic review scope. Also, CIs were promised in methods but not reported in Table 3. The paper is published in 'Information' journal, which is unusual for this topic area.
